title: Nigeria: uneven and unreliable access
source: World Bank, Development Data Group. Space2Stats (accessed: February 20, 2026).
visdescription: Map of nightlights in Nigeria as a proxy for electricity access.

scenes.text: [emphasis: Electricity access in Nigeria is highly uneven]. Southern zones enjoy relatively high coverage, while the north lags far behind. These disparities reflect the concentration of grid infrastructure and population in the south, along with differences in investment, urbanization, and political stability, as conflict has disrupted large parts of the north in recent years.

[emphasis: Nighttime lights provide a more granular view of electricity access]. They are a proxy for electricity access and usage. When overlaid on the map, they show where power is being consumed.[footnote: Nighttime lights only capture solar electricity use when backed up by batteries, as the panels do not produce electricity at night. They are therefore a closer proxy of grid connection and use.]

Thebrightest clusters correspond to major cities, such as Lagos, Abuja, and Port Harcourt, where grid access is widespread and power consumption relatively high. Vast rural and peri-urban areas show as weak or dark, signaling either a lack of access or unreliable supply.
Let’s focus on Nigeria’s[emphasis:  urban areas]…

… and zoom in on Kano. As in most cities, a large majority of households here are connected to the grid. Where bright lights are detected, neighborhoods are likely well served with access to reliable electricity.

Filtering out the brightest data points highlights that, even when connected to the grid, [emphasis: large urban and suburban zones may remain underserved, as nighttime lights appear ][emphasis: weak][emphasis: or ][emphasis: dark][emphasis: .]
Although these are densely populated suburban settlements, they still lack highly visible illuminations.

For example, this 36 square kilometer cell is estimated to house more than 100,000 people, but remains largely in the dark. This illustrates that, even though most urban areas are connected to the grid, quality of access is not yet universal and [emphasis: grid reliability is far from consistent].
